title: Photography
intro: Photography questions cluster around the medium's early fight for artistic legitimacy and its subsequent documentary turn. Alfred Stieglitz and Edward Steichen anchor the Photo-Secession and Pictorialist movement that sought to establish photography as fine art, while Paul Strand's shift toward sharply focused "straight photography" marks the transition into the documentary era. That documentary tradition dominates the mid-20th-century canon: Dorothea Lange, Walker Evans, and Margaret Bourke-White chronicled the Great Depression and rural poverty under the Farm Security Administration, with Lange's "Migrant Mother" as the era's single most recognizable image.

Photojournalism and war photography form a closely related cluster, led by Robert Capa and Henri Cartier-Bresson, both co-founders of the Magnum Photos cooperative, alongside Bourke-White's wartime work and the broader coverage of World War II and Vietnam. A smaller cluster covers experimental and avant-garde photography, led by Man Ray's Dadaist and Surrealist techniques and Brassai's atmospheric nighttime images of Paris. Portrait and street photography spans a wide range, from Julia Margaret Cameron's soft-focus 19th-century portraits to Diane Arbus's unflinching studies of marginalized subjects and Weegee's stark tabloid crime-scene images, while fashion and celebrity photography, represented by Annie Leibovitz and Vogue magazine, rounds out the unit's more contemporary end. Cities and landmarks — New York, Paris, Yosemite — recur as organizing answerlines tied to the photographers and movements associated with them.

sections.name: Pictorialism and the Photo-Secession
sections.blurb: Early-20th-century efforts to establish photography as a fine art rather than mere documentation.

entries.note: American photographer who founded the Photo-Secession movement and Camera Work journal; married to painter Georgia O'Keeffe.
Photographer who helped found the Photo-Secession with Stieglitz; later directed MoMA's photography department and curated The Family of Man.
Photographer who moved from Stieglitz's circle toward sharply focused "straight photography."

sections.name: Documentary and Depression-Era Photography
sections.blurb: Photographers who chronicled American poverty and rural life, especially under the Farm Security Administration.

entries.note: FSA photographer famed for "Migrant Mother," documenting Depression-era poverty and Japanese American internment.
1930s economic crisis documented extensively by Farm Security Administration photographers like Lange, Evans, and Bourke-White.
FSA photographer known for stark, formally precise images of rural poverty, notably in Let Us Now Praise Famous Men.
Danish-American photojournalist whose How the Other Half Lives exposed New York tenement poverty using early flash photography.
Dorothea Lange's iconic FSA photograph of a careworn mother, Florence Owens Thompson, and her children.

sections.name: Photojournalism and War Photography
sections.blurb: Photographers who documented conflict and world events for the press.

entries.note: First female war photojournalist for Life magazine; photographed Soviet industry, the Depression, and the WWII liberation of concentration camps.
French photographer who coined the "decisive moment" concept; co-founded the Magnum Photos cooperative.
Hungarian-American war photographer who covered the Spanish Civil War and D-Day; co-founded Magnum Photos.
Photographer-owned cooperative agency founded by Capa, Cartier-Bresson, and others to retain rights over their images.
Conflict documented by photojournalists including Robert Capa and Margaret Bourke-White.

sections.name: Surrealist and Avant-Garde Photography
sections.blurb: Experimental technique and imagery drawing on Dada and Surrealist art movements.

entries.note: American Dadaist and Surrealist photographer known for cameraless "rayographs" and the solarization technique.
Hungarian-French photographer known for atmospheric nighttime images of 1930s Paris, published as Paris de nuit.

sections.name: Street Photography and Portraiture
sections.blurb: Candid and posed images of people, from Victorian portraiture to unvarnished street observation.

entries.note: 19th-century British portrait photographer known for soft-focus images of Victorian luminaries and allegorical scenes.
American photographer known for unflinching portraits of marginalized and unconventional subjects.
American tabloid photographer known for stark flash images of New York crime scenes and nightlife.

sections.name: Fashion and Celebrity Photography
sections.blurb: Studio and editorial photography built around style, magazines, and public figures.

entries.note: Contemporary portrait photographer known for staged celebrity images for Rolling Stone and Vanity Fair.
Influential fashion magazine that showcased major photographers including Steichen and Leibovitz.

sections.name: Places, Institutions, and Motifs
sections.blurb: Cities, landmarks, and general vocabulary that recur across photographic movements.

entries.note: Home to the Bauhaus's experimental photography program and New Objectivity photographers like August Sander.
Home to Stieglitz's 291 gallery, the Photo-Secession, and countless street photographers.
Hub of early street photography, home to Brassai's nighttime images and Man Ray's Surrealist circle.
Frequently photographed New York City landmark designed by Frederick Law Olmsted.
General medium of capturing images through light-sensitive processes, spanning documentary, art, and journalistic traditions.
19th-century British art movement whose aesthetic influenced early portrait photographers like Julia Margaret Cameron.
Subject of extensive war photojournalism, including Nick Ut's and Eddie Adams's iconic Vietnam War images.
Landscape frequently photographed by Ansel Adams, known for his dramatic black-and-white images.
